title: The confusion matrix: precision, recall and the accuracy trap
intro: A classifier has four possible outcomes, and collapsing them into one "share of correct answers" is dangerous. To measure quality honestly, the outcomes are laid out in a confusion matrix, and metrics are computed from it to fit the specific task. Move the threshold and the class separability — all the numbers recompute.

beats.text: Every prediction lands in one of four cells. TP (true positive) — caught spam. TN (true negative) — correctly let a regular email through. FP (false positive) — wrongly marked a real email as spam. FN (false negative) — missed spam into the inbox. These four numbers ARE the confusion matrix.

Metrics are computed from the matrix, and the first two are the key ones. Precision: of everything marked as spam, what share really is spam — TP / (TP + FP). Recall: of all the real spam, what share was caught — TP / (TP + FN).
beats.predict: Move the threshold left (marking spam more generously). What happens to precision and recall?
beats.reveal: Recall grows (we catch more spam) while precision falls (we snag real emails along with the spam). Moving the threshold, you always trade one for the other — you cannot raise both at once, only pick a balance.

beats.text: The same numbers also go by their "medical" names. TPR (true positive rate) IS recall: the share of the target class caught. FPR (false positive rate) is the share of false alarms among the "clean" ones: FP / (FP + TN). Remember the TPR/FPR pair — the ROC curve grows out of it in the next lesson.

beats.predict: The classes overlap less. What happens to precision and recall simultaneously?
beats.reveal: Both grow. When the classes separate well, one good threshold is enough to catch nearly all the spam and barely touch the real mail. The precision/recall trade-off never disappears, but it softens — a good model pushes both metrics up.

beats.text: The main trap is accuracy, the share of correct answers (TP + TN) / total. On balanced classes it is reasonable, but on a rare class it deceives brutally. If spam is only 1%, the model "nothing is spam" is right 99% of the time — accuracy is huge, recall is zero, usefulness is zero. That is why on rare classes you watch precision and recall, not overall accuracy.

Which metric to choose is dictated by the cost of the error. Losing an important email hurts → protect precision (threshold higher). Missing a fraudulent transaction or a disease is dangerous → sacrifice precision for recall (threshold lower). There is no universal answer: first decide what costs more — a false alarm or a miss — and tune the threshold to that.

definitions.text: the 2×2 table of outcomes: TP, FP, FN, TN — correctly/incorrectly caught and missed objects of each class.
of those marked as the class — what share is correct. Falls when we snag extras.
of the whole target class — what share was caught. Falls when we miss.
what share of "clean" objects we wrongly marked as the class.
the share of correct answers. Deceptive on imbalanced classes.

practice: The first thing done with a trained model is building the confusion matrix and looking at precision/recall, not a single accuracy. Especially if the class of interest is rare: fraud, disease, churn. There, high accuracy means almost nothing.

When one number is needed to compare models, F1 is used — the harmonic mean of precision and recall: it is high only when both metrics are high. But even F1 is chosen deliberately, remembering that precision and recall answer different questions.
realLife: Medical screening is tuned for high recall: better to re-check a healthy person than to miss a sick one. That is why a sensitive first test is followed by a precise confirmatory one — it recovers the precision.

A spam filter, by contrast, protects precision: the user cares more about not losing a real email than about occasionally seeing spam in the inbox. The same confusion matrix, but opposite priorities — and the threshold sits in different places.
assumptions: The matrix is computed at a specific threshold: move it — and precision and recall change. Comparing models "at the default threshold of 0.5" is incorrect, especially on imbalanced classes.

Precision depends on the share of the positive class in the stream: move the model to where fraud is half as frequent — precision drops by itself, with no degradation of the model. Recall does not depend on the base rate.
nextLabel: How to judge a classifier at all thresholds at once? → The ROC curve and AUC
